QF.id: Taxation of Debt and Equity Products_12

question.text: Neha purchased unlisted equity shares of a startup for Rs.2,00,000 on 1st June 2023. She sold them on 15th August 2025 for Rs.5,00,000. What is the nature and rate of tax on her capital gain?

answer.text: Short-term capital gain at 20% under Section 111A
Long-term capital gain at 12.5% under Section 112 since holding exceeds 24 months
Short-term capital gain at slab rate since unlisted shares are always short-term under Section 50AA
Long-term capital gain at 12.5% under Section 112A with Rs.1,25,000 exemption

explanation.text: **The Logic:** For unlisted equity shares, the holding period for LTCG is 24 months. Neha held from June 2023 to August 2025 = 26 months > 24 months, so this is LTCG. Unlisted equity shares attract LTCG at 12.5% under Section 112 (not Section 112A, which applies only to listed equity with STT). Gain = Rs.5,00,000 - Rs.2,00,000 = Rs.3,00,000. Tax = 12.5% of Rs.3,00,000 = Rs.37,500. **The Trap:** Option C is designed for candidates who confuse unlisted EQUITY shares with unlisted BONDS. Section 50AA applies to unlisted bonds/debentures and specified MFs — NOT to unlisted equity shares. Option D tempts candidates who know 12.5% is correct but wrongly apply Section 112A (which is for listed equity with STT and includes the Rs.1,25,000 exemption). **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A and Series XV test the distinction between Section 112 and Section 112A frequently. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Unlisted equity shares have a different tax architecture from unlisted bonds. Shares get LTCG at 12.5% after 24 months (under s.112); bonds are always STCG (under s.50AA). Never conflate the two when advising clients on private placements.
